content:
4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

What the interviewer told meIf you’re a fresh graduate who just finished college, spent months binging software development bootcamps, and built projects via freeCodeCamp or Udemy — I know exactly how you feel. You think you’re finally ready to apply for that first Software Engineer job. Right?
Well , you are kinda true and that's what I exactly did . I wanted the flexibility of freelancing, so I jumped onto Upwork. But finding clients was brutal.
Eventually, I caught a break. I landed my first client.
Spoiler alert: It was an absolute mess. Imposter syndrome completely took the steering wheel. I was so anxious to please them that I didn’t even make them initiate an official contract. I just put my head down and did the work.
You can probably guess what happened next. I handed over the project, and the client vanished into thin air. The silence was louder than anything. I had just worked for free.
It hurt, but I refused to quit. I adjusted my approach, learned from my mistakes, and kept applying for gigs. And guess what?
It worked. It actually worked.
Eventually, things turned around. I became an Upwork “Rising Talent” and started earning decent money. But honestly? It wasn’t satisfying.
I felt completely isolated.
That’s the downside of freelancing that nobody warns you about: the crushing loneliness. There are no coworkers, no coffee chats, and no office culture. It’s just you, your laptop, and the code, staring at each other in a quiet room.
I realized I needed a change. To fight the loneliness, I decided to pivot and find a physical, on-site role. I needed to be around people.
So, I started the hunt. I deliberately avoided LinkedIn — it felt way too saturated, like throwing my resume into a black hole with thousands of other applicants. Instead, I tried different job platforms.
And then, it finally happened.
My phone buzzed. I landed my very first official interview for a Junior Software Engineer role.
The process was split into three stages.
First up was the standard HR screening. I introduced myself, gave a quick overview of my university projects, and passed with flying colors.
Next up was the phase I dreaded the most: the technical interview.
If you’ve applied for dev roles, you know every company has its own “vibe” here. Some grill you on complex data structures and LeetCode algorithms. Others don’t. This company took a completely different route.
The recruiter asked me to build a complete system from scratch, design the database and Entity-Relationship Diagram (ERD), and present it all via a PowerPoint presentation.
It was a lot of work, but I put my head down and built exactly what they asked for. There was just one massive problem…
Here was the catch: my time freelancing on Upwork had completely warped how I coded.
Freelance clients want results fast. In fact, many of mine explicitly told me to use AI to speed things up. Because I relied so heavily on AI to write code over the past few months, my actual coding muscle memory had gotten incredibly rusty.
Don’t get me wrong — I revised my SQL questions. I nailed the database theory. I understood the concepts perfectly. But when it came to actually translating those thoughts through my own fingertips without an AI autocomplete?
My mind completely locked up.
The interviewer looked at my system and asked for a simple live coding modification: add a new column to a table and write a basic CRUD function for it.
Simple, right? Except my folder structure had been generated by Cursor. I literally couldn’t find where the CRUD functions were supposed to go. Five painful minutes ticked by as I stumbled through my own project files.
Finally, the interviewer stopped me. He looked at the screen and said, “Your dependency on AI is as clear as the sun.”
Ouch. It was a brutal realization. I felt like the interview was completely over.
But then, the most interesting part happened…
He looked at me, paused, and then threw a curveball.
Instead of a rejection letter, he offered me a 6-week paid internship with a direct path to a full-time contract.
To be completely honest, a massive wave of relief washed over me. But at the same time, a dark cloud followed me around for the next few days. I couldn’t shake the bitter taste of failing at the most basic coding task because I had let AI do the thinking for me.
It was a humbling reality check, but also the best thing that could have happened. It taught me a vital lesson that every junior developer entering the market needs to hear:
AI tools like Cursor and ChatGPT are incredible co-pilots, but you still need to know how to fly the plane yourself.
If you’re a fresh graduate relying heavily on AI to build your portfolio, step away from the autocomplete every once in a world. Make sure your hands still know how to write the code, because when you’re in the hot seat, the screen doesn’t lie.
Now, it’s time for me to put my head down, log out of the AI editors for a bit, and crush these six weeks.
